curriculum.title: Becoming an AI Operator
curriculum.subtitle: Mental model, Desktop Commander, first demos, Projects
curriculum.notes: Completed: Claude Max signup, Desktop Commander + Filesystem connectors installed, organized contracts/agreements folder, photo-to-structured-data (handwritten note digitized to DOCX), Google Calendar + Drive + Gmail connectors enabled, created AI Native Operator Training project in Cowork with CLAUDE.md, scheduled Session 2 via natural language calendar integration. Git for Windows install started but needs verification.
keyTakeaways: Claude Desktop with connectors = Claude has hands on your machine
Cowork mode handles desktop automation (file ops, browser, apps)
Projects + CLAUDE.md = persistent memory across conversations
Context window degrades past 75% — use session handoffs
Caps Lock = voice input for hands-free interaction

references.title: Hardware Overview
references.subtitle: Recommended machine specs and setup for AI-native work

sections.heading: Recommended Machine
sections.body: Mac Mini M4 Pro (16 GB RAM, 512 GB SSD) is the sweet spot for AI-native operator work. Quiet, capable, and designed to stay on your desk 24/7. The unified memory architecture handles large Claude contexts without the thermal throttling you'd see on a MacBook under sustained load.

sections.heading: Required Peripherals
sections.body: The Mac Mini ships without a display, keyboard, or mouse. You'll need a monitor with HDMI or DisplayPort input. Reuse what you have or budget roughly $200 for a decent 1080p/1440p display. Any USB or Bluetooth keyboard and mouse works.

sections.heading: Why Mac?
sections.body: Claude Desktop and Claude Code are both first-class on macOS. The macOS terminal is Unix-based, which maps cleanly to Claude Code's expectations and the broader developer toolchain (Homebrew, Git, Python, Node). Less friction than Windows for the terminal-heavy work introduced in Session 2 onward.

sections.heading: Windows Note
sections.body: If you're on Windows (like Matt), everything in the curriculum works — Claude Desktop, Claude Code via PowerShell, Git, and MCP connectors. The main differences: terminal commands use PowerShell syntax, and you'll use winget or direct installers instead of Homebrew. The Setup Guide PDF covers the Windows-specific install paths in full.

references.title: Software Overview
references.subtitle: The AI operator stack — what each tool does and how they connect

sections.heading: The Mental Model: Brain, Hands, Memory
sections.body: Every AI operator setup has three layers. Claude (via Desktop or the API) is the Brain — it thinks, reasons, writes, and plans. Claude Code is the Hands — it executes terminal commands, writes files, runs code, and builds things end-to-end. CLAUDE.md is the Memory — a plain text file that persists your business context, conventions, and preferences across every session. Master these three layers and you can automate almost anything.

sections.heading: Claude Desktop
sections.body: The chat interface most people start with — but far more powerful than it looks. Claude Desktop supports Projects (persistent context that survives between sessions), Cowork mode (browser and filesystem control via the computer-use API), and MCP connectors that wire Claude into your real tools: Google Calendar, Gmail, Drive, Notion, and dozens more. Use it for thinking-heavy work, document analysis, strategy, and anything that benefits from a conversation thread.

sections.heading: Claude Code
sections.body: A terminal-based agentic loop that can read your codebase, write and edit files, run shell commands, and build complete software from a plain-English prompt. You describe what you want; Claude Code plans, executes, checks its own output, and iterates. Session 2 covers this in depth. For now: it's the tool that makes 'build me an app' actually work.

sections.heading: MCP Connectors
sections.body: Model Context Protocol (MCP) is the plugin system that lets Claude reach into your actual tools. With Google Calendar connected, Claude can read your schedule and book meetings. With Gmail, it can draft and search email. With Filesystem, it can read and write files on your machine. Each connector is installed once via Claude Desktop settings and is then available in every conversation going forward.

sections.heading: Cowork Mode
sections.body: A special capability in Claude Desktop that gives Claude control of your browser and desktop apps. You can say 'go to our CRM and pull the last 5 provider applications' and it will navigate, click, read, and report back. Powerful for tasks that live in GUIs rather than APIs — no code required on your end.

tasks.title: Install Git for Windows
tasks.description: Download from git-scm.com, install with defaults ('Add Git to PATH' stays checked), verify with 'git --version' in a fresh PowerShell window.

tasks.title: Verify Claude Code in terminal
tasks.description: Fresh PowerShell → 'claude --version'. If not recognized, run: irm https://claude.ai/install.ps1 | iex — then close and reopen terminal.

tasks.title: Build tip calculator (Code tab)
tasks.description: Claude Desktop → Code tab → 'Build a tip calculator app with bill splitting. Clean design, mobile-friendly. Save to Desktop and open in browser.'

tasks.title: Build tip calculator (Terminal)
tasks.description: PowerShell → mkdir $HOME\ai-demo → cd $HOME\ai-demo → claude → same prompt. Experience the terminal workflow difference.

tasks.title: Create 'HouseAccount Builder' project
tasks.description: Claude Desktop → Projects → New → 'HouseAccount Builder'. Add instructions describing HA, your role, team, the 4 agents, and that you're on Windows.

tasks.title: 3 real tasks per day in Claude
tasks.description: Provider emails, customer analysis, document review — actual HouseAccount work. Use Chat or Cowork, whichever feels natural.

tasks.title: Upload a dense document for analysis
tasks.description: Drop in a vendor contract, long email chain, or competitor's terms. Ask Claude to extract the 3 things that matter and flag anything unusual.

tasks.title: Try Caps Lock voice input
tasks.description: Hold Caps Lock → talk a real problem → let go. Use it for at least one actual task this week. Notice how different it feels.

mantras: The models are intelligence. When you're doing something that requires thinking, that's a signal to test a model with it.
Brain, Hands, Memory. Claude thinks. Claude Code acts. CLAUDE.md remembers.
If you're doing it more than twice, it should be a prompt. If it's a prompt you run more than twice, it should be automated.
Context is everything. A model with no context is smart but useless. A model with your business context is a teammate.
You're not learning a tool. You're building a new reflex.
The best AI operator isn't the best prompter. It's the person who knows what to hand off and what to keep.
